3-1.q: Variance analysis is Primarily used to:
o: Compare actual results to budgeted amounts and identify differences
3-1.e: Variance analysis = comparing Actual to Budget to identify differences (variances), investigate causes, and take corrective action. It is a management control tool, not for tax or pricing purposes.

3-1.q: Management by exception means managers:
o: Focus attention on significant variances that exceed a threshold
3-1.e: Management by exception: focus on Significant deviations from budget — both favorable and unfavorable. Routine, immaterial variances are not investigated, allowing efficient use of management time.

3-1.q: The Most commonly used level of activity for setting standard costs is:
3-1.e: Practical standards are most commonly used — they reflect achievable performance under normal conditions, including expected downtime, waste, and learning. Ideal standards are unachievable; historical may perpetuate inefficiencies.

3-1.q: A Favorable cost variance means:
3-1.e: For Costs: Favorable = actual cost is Less than standard/budget. The company spent less than planned. For Revenue: Favorable = actual revenue is More than budget.

3-1.q: Level 2 variance analysis separates:
3-1.e: Level 2 splits the static budget variance into: (1) Flexible Budget Variance (price/efficiency differences) and (2) Sales Volume Variance (volume differences). This is more informative than Level 1.

3-1.q: The Sales Volume Variance is calculated as:
3-1.e: Sales Volume Variance = Flexible Budget − Static Budget. It shows the impact of selling more or fewer units than planned, holding price and efficiency constant.

3-1.q: Fixed costs in a flexible budget:
o: Remain the same within the relevant range
3-1.e: Fixed costs remain Constant within the relevant range in a flexible budget. Only Variable costs are recalculated at the actual activity level. Fixed costs stay at the budgeted amount.

3-1.q: Benchmarking as a source for standards compares performance to:
3-1.e: Benchmarking = comparing to Best-In-Class industry performance to set ambitious but realistic standards. Encourages competitiveness but may not perfectly fit every company's situation.

3-1.q: Level 3 variance analysis provides the Most detail and includes:
o: Price, efficiency, mix, and yield variances for each manufacturing input
3-1.e: Level 3 is the Most Detailed — it breaks down variances for each manufacturing input (DM, DL, OH) into price, efficiency, mix, and yield components.

3-1.q: Activity Analysis as a standard-setting source is Best described as:
o: Using past performance as the benchmark
Engineering studies of tasks and required resources
Setting costs based on market price
3-1.e: Activity Analysis = engineering studies that analyze each task and resource requirement scientifically. Most Accurate method but also most Time-Consuming and Costly.

3-1.q: Level 1 variance analysis compares:
o: Actual vs static (master) budget — one total variance
3-1.e: Level 1 = Total Static Budget Variance = Actual Results vs Static (Master) Budget. It is the broadest, least informative level — just total difference. Level 2 splits it into flexible budget variance + sales volume variance for more insight.

3-1.q: The Static Budget Variance for costs = Actual Costs − Static Budget Costs. If the result is Positive:
o: Unfavorable — actual costs Exceeded the static budget
It depends on the variance type
3-1.e: For Costs, a positive static budget variance = Unfavorable (spent More than budgeted). For Revenues, a positive variance = Favorable (earned more than budgeted). Always remember the direction differs for costs vs revenues.

3-1.q: The Flexible Budget Variance tells us:
o: Whether we sold more or fewer units than planned
Whether costs and prices were Efficient given the Actual level of activity
The total difference between actual and budget
3-1.e: Flexible Budget Variance = Actual Results vs Flexible Budget (at actual volume). It isolates Price and Efficiency effects — was the company efficient given the actual activity level? It removes the volume effect.

3-1.q: The Sales Volume Variance tells us:
o: Whether the selling price was above budget
How much of the total variance is due to selling More or Fewer units than planned
3-1.e: Sales Volume Variance = Flexible Budget vs Static Budget. It isolates the Volume Effect — how much did profit change because actual units sold differed from the budget? No price or efficiency assumptions are changed.

3-1.q: Ideal (theoretical) standards are rarely used for budgeting because:
o: They are too easy to achieve
They create always-Unfavorable variances since perfection is unattainable in practice
3-1.e: Ideal standards assume Perfect conditions (zero waste, zero downtime). In practice, they are unachievable. Comparing actual to ideal Always produces unfavorable variances, which demoralizes employees and makes variances meaningless for control purposes.

3-1.q: An Unfavorable revenue variance means:
o: Actual revenue was Less than budgeted revenue
3-1.e: Unfavorable revenue variance = Actual Revenue < Budgeted Revenue. Less revenue than planned. The opposite of cost variances — for revenues, higher actual = favorable; lower actual = unfavorable.

3-1.q: Standard costs serve all of the following purposes Except:
o: Replacing actual cost accounting for GAAP reporting
3-1.e: Standard costs serve budgeting, pricing, and performance evaluation. However, GAAP requires reporting at Actual costs for external financial statements. Standard costs are an Internal management tool — they cannot replace actual cost accounting for external reporting.

3-1.q: The flexible budget is prepared at:
o: The budgeted activity level (same as static budget)
The Actual activity level achieved during the period
3-1.e: The flexible budget is Recalculated at the Actual Activity Level. This allows fair performance comparison: actual costs vs what Should have been spent at that same level of activity — removing volume-driven differences.

3-1.q: Level 2 variance analysis breaks the Static Budget Variance into:
o: Flexible Budget Variance + Sales Volume Variance
3-1.e: Level 2 splits the total static budget variance into: (1) Flexible Budget Variance (price/efficiency at actual volume) + (2) Sales Volume Variance (effect of volume difference). This is more informative than the Level 1 total.

3-1.q: The purpose of Standard Costs is to:
o: Replace actual costing for inventory valuation on GAAP statements
Serve as Benchmarks for planning, pricing, and performance evaluation
Eliminate the need for variance analysis
3-1.e: Standard costs are Predetermined benchmarks representing expected costs under normal conditions. They are used for: (1) Budgeting (planning), (2) Pricing (cost-based pricing), and (3) Performance Evaluation (variance analysis). They are an internal management tool.

3-2.q: DM Price Variance = (AP − SP) × Aq. If AP = $8, SP = $10, Aq = 5,000 lbs, variance =
3-2.e: DM Price Variance = ($8 − $10) × 5,000 = −$2 × 5,000 = −$10,000 = $10,000 Favorable (paid less per lb than standard).

3-2.q: DM Quantity Variance = (Aq − Sq) × SP. Aq = 6,200 lbs, Sq Allowed = 6,000 lbs, SP = $5. Variance =
3-2.e: DM Qty Variance = (6,200 − 6,000) × $5 = 200 × $5 = $1,000 Unfavorable (used More material than standard allowed).

3-2.q: Standard quantity Allowed for actual output is calculated as:
o: Standard qty per unit × Actual units produced
Standard qty per unit × Budgeted units
3-2.e: Standard Quantity Allowed = Standard qty per unit × Actual units produced. This is the benchmark — how much material Should have been used to produce the actual output.

3-2.q: The DM Price Variance is typically controlled by:
3-2.e: The Purchasing manager controls the DM Price Variance — they negotiate supplier contracts and make buying decisions. The production manager controls the Quantity Variance (how much material is used in production).

3-2.q: An Unfavorable DM Quantity Variance could be caused by:
o: Using less material through better design
Abnormal spoilage and waste in production
3-2.e: Unfavorable DM Quantity Variance = used More material than standard. Causes: poor quality materials causing waste, machine breakdowns, abnormal spoilage, inexperienced workers, theft.

3-2.q: Total DM Variance = $3,000 Unfavorable. DM Price Variance = $1,000 Favorable. DM Quantity Variance =
3-2.e: Price + Quantity = Total: $1,000 F + Qty = $3,000 U. Qty = $3,000 U − (−$1,000) = $4,000 Unfavorable.

3-2.q: The Materials Purchase Price Variance differs from DM Price Variance because it uses:
3-2.e: Purchase Price Variance = (AP − SP) × Qty Purchased (not used). This allows immediate identification of purchasing inefficiency when materials arrive, rather than waiting until they are used in production.

3-2.q: A Favorable DM Price Variance combined with an Unfavorable DM Quantity Variance might indicate:
o: Cheaper but lower-quality materials were purchased, requiring more usage
Workers are inefficient and prices are high
3-2.e: Buying Cheaper materials (favorable price) may result in using More material (unfavorable quantity) due to lower quality causing waste, rework, or spoilage. These variances often have a cause-and-effect relationship.

3-2.q: Standard: 2 lbs per unit at $6/lb. Actual production: 1,000 units. Actual usage: 2,100 lbs at $5.50/lb. DM Quantity Variance =
3-2.e: Sq Allowed = 2 × 1,000 = 2,000 lbs. Qty Variance = (2,100 − 2,000) × $6 = 100 × $6 = $600 Unfavorable.

3-2.q: Manufacturing input variances are based on units:
3-2.e: Manufacturing input variances (DM, DL, OH) are based on units Produced — they are production variances, not sales variances. They appear on the production variance report, not the income statement.

3-2.q: DM Price Variance formula in words:
3-2.e: DM Price Variance = (Actual Price − Standard Price) × Actual Quantity. Measures the price Paid vs the standard price. Multiply by actual quantity to isolate the pure price effect.

3-2.q: DM Quantity Variance formula in words:
3-2.e: DM Quantity Variance = (Actual Quantity Used − Standard Quantity Allowed) × Standard Price. Uses Standard price to isolate the Quantity (efficiency) effect — removing any price differences.

3-2.q: A Favorable DM Price Variance most likely indicates:
o: Poor quality materials were purchased at lower than standard price
More units were produced than planned
The purchasing manager exceeded volume targets
3-2.e: Favorable DM Price Variance = paid Less than standard price. Most common cause: purchased lower-quality or cheaper materials. Warning: cheap materials may cause Unfavorable DM Quantity Variance (more waste). Always analyze price and quantity variances together.

3-2.q: The DM Quantity Variance uses Standard Price (not actual price) because:
o: Standard price is easier to calculate
It isolates Only the quantity/efficiency effect, removing any price differences
3-2.e: Using Standard Price in the Quantity Variance isolates Pure Efficiency — was more or less material used than the standard? If actual price were used, any price change would contaminate the efficiency measurement.

3-2.q: Standard: 2 lbs per unit at $6/lb. Actual output: 1,000 units. Actual DM used: 2,100 lbs. Sq Allowed =
3-2.e: Sq Allowed = Standard qty per unit × Actual units produced = 2 lbs × 1,000 = 2,000 lbs. This is the Benchmark — how much material Should have been used for 1,000 units.

3-2.q: Standard qty allowed (Sq) = 2,000 lbs. Actual qty used (Aq) = 2,100 lbs. Standard price (SP) = $6/lb. DM Quantity Variance =
3-2.e: DM Quantity Variance = (Aq − Sq) × SP = (2,100 − 2,000) × $6 = 100 × $6 = $600 Unfavorable. Used 100 lbs more than standard → Unfavorable.

3-2.q: Cheap materials causing excess waste would produce:
o: Favorable price And favorable quantity variances
Favorable price variance But potentially unfavorable quantity variance
Unfavorable price And unfavorable quantity variances
3-2.e: Cheap materials: Favorable price variance (paid less). But cheap/lower quality materials may generate More Waste in production → Unfavorable quantity variance. Management must evaluate both variances together — a favorable price may actually cost more overall.

3-2.q: The Materials Purchase Price Variance is computed at time of Purchase rather than use because:
o: It enables Earlier identification of purchasing inefficiency when materials arrive
3-2.e: Recording the Purchase Price Variance at Purchase provides Earlier information — purchasing managers know immediately whether they paid above or below standard, allowing faster corrective action than waiting until materials are used in production.

3-2.q: If actual price = standard price but actual quantity used exceeds standard quantity, the result is:
o: Zero price variance and Unfavorable quantity variance
3-2.e: When AP = SP → DM Price Variance = Zero. When Aq > Sq → DM Quantity Variance = Unfavorable (used more material than standard). Total DM variance = unfavorable (only driven by quantity inefficiency).

3-2.q: The Purchasing manager is Most likely responsible for which variance?
3-2.e: The Purchasing Manager controls DM Price Variance — they negotiate supplier prices and select vendors. The Production Manager controls DM Quantity Variance (how much material is used in the factory). Matching responsibility to the right manager is essential for accountability.

3-3.q: DL Rate Variance = (Ar − Sr) × AH. Ar = $18, Sr = $16, AH = 2,000 hrs. Variance =
3-3.e: DL Rate Variance = ($18 − $16) × 2,000 = $2 × 2,000 = $4,000 Unfavorable (paid more per hour than standard).

3-3.q: DL Efficiency Variance = (AH − Sh) × Sr. AH = 1,800 hrs, Sh allowed = 2,000 hrs, Sr = $15. Variance =
3-3.e: DL Efficiency Variance = (1,800 − 2,000) × $15 = −200 × $15 = −$3,000 = $3,000 Favorable (used Fewer hours than standard — better efficiency).

3-3.q: Standard: 3 DL hours per unit at $12/hr. Actual: produced 500 units, worked 1,600 hours. Standard hours allowed =
3-3.e: Standard Hours Allowed = Standard hours per unit × Actual units produced = 3 × 500 = 1,500 hours.

3-3.q: An Unfavorable DL Efficiency Variance could be caused by:
o: Skilled workers finishing faster than expected
3-3.e: Unfavorable DL Efficiency Variance = used More hours than standard. Causes include equipment breakdowns, poor scheduling, lower-quality materials causing rework, inexperienced workers.

3-3.q: DL Rate Variance is Primarily controlled by:
3-3.e: The DL Rate Variance is controlled by HR (negotiating wage rates) and payroll. The production manager controls the Efficiency Variance (how many hours workers take to complete production).

3-3.q: Total DL Variance = $2,000 Unfavorable. Rate Variance = $5,000 Unfavorable. Efficiency Variance =
3-3.e: Rate + Efficiency = Total: $5,000 U + Efficiency = $2,000 U. Efficiency = $2,000 U − $5,000 U = −$3,000 = $3,000 Favorable.

3-3.q: The DL Efficiency Variance uses the Standard rate (not actual rate) to:
o: Isolate the efficiency effect from wage rate changes
3-3.e: Using Standard rate in the Efficiency Variance isolates Pure Efficiency — how many hours were used vs. standard, valued at the same rate. If actual rate were used, a rate change would contaminate the efficiency measurement.

3-3.q: Using lower-skilled workers at lower hourly rates might cause:
o: A favorable rate variance but possibly unfavorable efficiency variance
3-3.e: Lower-skilled workers may be Cheaper (favorable rate variance) but Slower (unfavorable efficiency variance) due to lower productivity. These variances often have an inverse relationship.

3-3.q: Standard: 0.5 hrs/unit at $20/hr. Produced 4,000 units. Actual: 2,200 hrs at $19/hr. Total DL Variance =
The DL Efficiency Variance measures:
o: Whether the wage rate paid was above or below standard
Whether workers used more or fewer hours than standard for actual output
The total DL cost vs budget
3-3.e: DL Efficiency Variance measures Labor Time Efficiency — whether workers took more or fewer hours than the standard allowed for the actual output produced.

3-3.q: DL Rate Variance formula:
3-3.e: DL Rate Variance = (Actual Rate − Standard Rate) × Actual Hours. Measures the Wage Rate paid vs standard. Multiply by actual hours to isolate the pure rate effect.

3-3.q: DL Efficiency Variance formula:
3-3.e: DL Efficiency Variance = (Actual Hours − Standard Hours Allowed) × Standard Rate. Uses Standard Rate to isolate the Efficiency effect — were more or fewer hours used than standard? Standard rate removes any wage rate differences.

3-3.q: Overtime pay for workers causes which variance?
o: DL Rate Variance — unfavorable (overtime premium increases the average rate)
3-3.e: Overtime premium increases the Average Actual Rate paid per hour → Unfavorable DL Rate Variance. If overtime results from production rushing to meet demand, it may be a management decision (planned) or an efficiency problem (unplanned).

3-3.q: Using Higher-Skilled workers than the standard labor grade typically causes:
o: Unfavorable Rate Variance (higher wage) but potentially Favorable Efficiency Variance (faster work)
3-3.e: Higher-skill workers: Unfavorable Rate (paid more per hour than standard). But they may work Faster → Favorable Efficiency (fewer hours used). Management must evaluate both together — sometimes paying more per hour reduces total DL cost.

3-3.q: Idle time (workers paid but not working) most directly causes:
o: Unfavorable DL Efficiency Variance — hours paid but not productive
No DL variance — idle time is normal
3-3.e: Idle Time = workers are paid but not producing. Actual hours Worked (paid) > Standard hours Needed for output → Unfavorable DL Efficiency Variance. Idle time is a form of labor inefficiency that must be tracked and investigated.

3-3.q: Standard: 2 DL hours per unit at $14/hr. Actual: 500 units produced, 1,080 hours worked at $15/hr. Sh Allowed =
3-3.e: Sh Allowed = Standard hours per unit × Actual units = 2 × 500 = 1,000 hours. This is how many hours Should have been used to produce 500 units.

3-3.q: Actual hours worked (AH) = 1,080. Standard hours allowed (SH) = 1,000. Standard rate (SR) = $14/hr. DL Efficiency Variance =
3-3.e: DL Efficiency Variance = (AH − SH) × SR = (1,080 − 1,000) × $14 = 80 × $14 = $1,120 Unfavorable. Worked 80 more hours than standard → Unfavorable.

3-3.q: Actual rate paid (AR) = $15/hr. Standard rate (SR) = $14/hr. Actual hours worked (AH) = 1,080. DL Rate Variance =
3-3.e: DL Rate Variance = (AR − SR) × AH = ($15 − $14) × 1,080 = $1 × 1,080 = $1,080 Unfavorable. Paid $1 more per hour than standard → Unfavorable.

3-3.q: A Favorable DL Rate Variance could be caused by:
o: Using Lower-Paid workers than standard grade — but risks unfavorable efficiency
3-3.e: Favorable DL Rate Variance = paid Less than standard per hour. Typically from: using Lower-Skill workers (paid less) than the standard labor grade. Warning: cheaper workers may take longer → Unfavorable efficiency. Must evaluate both variances together.

3-3.q: The Production Manager is Most responsible for which variance?
o: DL Efficiency Variance — they supervise workers and control how long production takes
3-3.e: The Production Manager controls DL Efficiency — they supervise workers, schedule production, and manage the production process. They cannot control the wage rates HR negotiates (Rate Variance), but they Can control how efficiently workers are used.

3-4.q: Mix and Yield variances are calculated only when:
o: There is one type of material
More than one material type or labor class is used
3-4.e: Mix and Yield variances arise only when More Than One input type is used — either multiple raw materials blended together or multiple labor skill classes. With a single input, only Price and Quantity variances exist.

3-4.q: The Mix Variance measures:
o: Whether total input was above standard
Whether the proportion (blend) of inputs used differed from standard
The wage rate paid vs standard
3-4.e: Mix Variance = the effect of using a Different Proportion of inputs than the standard mix. If you used more of a cheaper input than standard, you get a favorable mix variance.

3-4.q: A Favorable Mix Variance means:
o: More expensive inputs were substituted for cheaper ones
Cheaper inputs were used in higher proportion than standard
3-4.e: Favorable Mix Variance = (WASP_AM < WASP_SM) = the Actual Mix has a Lower weighted average standard price than the standard mix. This means more Low-Cost inputs and fewer high-cost inputs were used.

3-4.q: The Yield Variance formula is:
3-4.e: Yield Variance = (Total Aq − Total Sq) × WASP_SM. It measures whether the Total quantity of all inputs used was more or less than the standard allowed for actual output.

3-4.q: If Total Quantity Variance = $8,000 Unfavorable and Mix Variance = $3,000 Favorable, Yield Variance =
3-4.e: Mix + Yield = Total Quantity Variance. $3,000 F + Yield = $8,000 U. Yield = $8,000 U + $3,000 U = $11,000 Unfavorable.

3-4.q: WASP_SM (Weighted Average Standard Price for Standard Mix) is calculated using:
o: Standard quantities allowed at standard prices
3-4.e: WASP_SM = total standard cost of all inputs at standard proportions ÷ total standard quantity. Uses Standard quantities and Standard prices — the benchmark mix.

3-4.q: An Unfavorable Yield Variance indicates:
o: A more expensive mix was used
More total inputs were used than standard allowed for actual output
The proportion of inputs was wrong
3-4.e: Unfavorable Yield Variance = Total Aq > Total Sq. More Total Inputs were used than the standard allowed for the actual output. This is a total quantity efficiency issue, separate from the mix proportion issue.

3-4.q: The relationship between Mix and Yield variances is:
3-4.e: Mix Variance + Yield Variance = Total Quantity (Efficiency) Variance. Together they explain the full quantity variance. Mix explains the Proportion change; Yield explains the Total Quantity change.

3-4.q: Multiple class labor variances occur when:
o: All workers earn the same rate
Different wage rates exist for different skill classes used in production
Only one type of worker is used
3-4.e: Multiple class labor variances arise when Different Skill Classes of workers with Different wage rates are used in production. The mix variance shows the effect of using a different skill mix than standard.

3-4.q: WASP_AM vs WASP_SM: if WASP_AM > WASP_SM, the Mix Variance is:
3-4.e: Mix Variance = (WASP_AM − WASP_SM) × Total Aq. If WASP_AM > WASP_SM (actual mix has higher weighted cost than standard mix), the Mix Variance is Positive = Unfavorable (using more expensive inputs than planned).

3-4.q: WASP_AM (Weighted Average Standard Price for Actual Mix) is calculated as:
o: Standard quantities at standard prices divided by total standard quantity
Actual quantities of each input at their Standard Prices divided by total actual quantity
Standard price of the most used material
3-4.e: WASP_AM = Sum of (Actual quantity of each input × Standard price of each input) / Total actual quantity. It is the weighted average standard price of the Actual blend used.

3-4.q: Mix Variance = (WASP_AM − WASP_SM) × Total Aq. A favorable Mix Variance means:
o: WASP_AM > WASP_SM — actual mix is more expensive
WASP_AM < WASP_SM — actual mix is Cheaper than standard
3-4.e: Favorable Mix Variance = WASP_AM < WASP_SM. The Actual Mix has a Lower weighted average standard price than the standard mix — meaning cheaper inputs were substituted in higher proportions. This saves cost on the mix but may affect yield.

3-4.q: If a company uses only One type of raw material, the Mix Variance is:
o: Zero — there is no mix to vary
3-4.e: With only One material, there is No Mix to vary. Mix Variance only arises when Multiple inputs are combined in some proportion. No mix → no mix variance. Only Price and Quantity variances exist.

3-4.q: An Unfavorable Yield Variance indicates:
o: A cheaper input mix was used
More total inputs were used than the standard quantity allowed for actual output
Fewer inputs were used than standard
3-4.e: Unfavorable Yield = Total Aq > Total Sq Allowed. More total inputs were consumed than the standard allows for the actual output produced. This is a productivity problem — the inputs are not being converted to output as efficiently as standard.

3-4.q: A Favorable Yield Variance indicates:
o: The mix was more expensive than standard
Total inputs used were Less than the standard allowed — better conversion efficiency
The actual blend had more cheap materials
3-4.e: Favorable Yield = Total Aq < Total Sq Allowed. Fewer total inputs were needed than the standard — the conversion process was More Efficient than expected. Could result from better equipment, skilled workers, or higher-quality inputs.

3-4.q: Labor Mix and Yield variances arise when:
o: Only one type of labor is used
Multiple skill classes of labor are used in a standard blend
3-4.e: Labor Mix and Yield variances arise when Multiple Labor Grades (skill classes) are used — e.g., senior and junior workers — in a standard proportion. The Mix Variance shows whether the proportion of grades used differed from standard.

3-4.q: Total Quantity Variance = Mix Variance + Yield Variance. Mix = $2,000 F, Yield = $5,000 U. Total Quantity Variance =
3-4.e: Total Qty Variance = $2,000 F + $5,000 U = −$2,000 + $5,000 = $3,000 Unfavorable. The cheap mix saved money but the poor yield cost more — net unfavorable.

3-4.q: A company substitutes cheaper Material B for expensive Material A in greater proportion. Mix Variance is favorable but Yield Variance is unfavorable. This means:
o: Saved money on mix but lost more through poor conversion — need to assess the net effect
3-4.e: Using cheaper material mix = Favorable Mix variance. But cheaper material may cause More Waste in conversion = Unfavorable Yield. Management must look at the Net effect: if yield loss > mix savings, the substitution was not beneficial overall.

3-4.q: Mix and Yield variances Subdivide which higher-level variance?
3-4.e: Mix and Yield variances are a Further Breakdown of the DM Quantity Variance. Quantity Variance shows whether total material usage was efficient. Mix shows the blend proportion effect; Yield shows the total efficiency effect. Together they equal the Quantity Variance.

3-4.q: The Yield Variance is considered an internal efficiency measure because it focuses on:
o: How well the company converts its inputs into outputs — a controllable production efficiency
Mix proportion decisions made by purchasing
3-4.e: Yield Variance measures Production Conversion Efficiency — how well inputs become outputs. It is primarily a Production/Operations responsibility. Better processes, equipment maintenance, and skilled supervision improve yield.

3-5.q: The VOH Efficiency Variance measures:
o: Whether variable overhead was spent efficiently per dollar
Whether the allocation base (e.g., DL hours) was used efficiently
The difference between actual and standard overhead rates
3-5.e: VOH Efficiency Variance = (Aq − Sq) × Standard VOH Rate. It measures efficiency in using the Allocation Base (e.g., DL hours). If DL hours are over budget, VOH efficiency is unfavorable — more base units used than standard.

3-5.q: FOH Production Volume Variance is Best described as:
o: The difference between actual and budgeted fixed costs
A measure of capacity utilization — how well fixed costs are absorbed
3-5.e: FOH Volume Variance = Budgeted FOH − Applied FOH. It measures Capacity Utilization — whether fixed costs were fully absorbed based on actual output vs planned output. Positive (U) = produced less than planned.

3-5.q: Actual VOH = $45,000. Applied VOH = $42,000. Total VOH Variance =
3-5.e: Total VOH Variance = Actual VOH − Applied VOH = $45,000 − $42,000 = $3,000 Unfavorable (actual incurred More than applied/absorbed).

3-5.q: Actual FOH = $50,000. Budgeted FOH = $48,000. FOH Spending Variance =
3-5.e: FOH Spending Variance = Actual FOH − Budgeted FOH = $50,000 − $48,000 = $2,000 Unfavorable (spent More on fixed costs than budgeted).

3-5.q: Budgeted FOH = $60,000. Applied FOH = $54,000. FOH Production Volume Variance =
3-5.e: FOH Volume Variance = Budgeted FOH − Applied FOH = $60,000 − $54,000 = $6,000 Unfavorable (applied Less than budgeted = produced fewer units than planned = under-absorbed fixed overhead).

3-5.q: An Unfavorable FOH Production Volume Variance indicates:
o: Actual production was Below planned production (under-absorbed)
3-5.e: Unfavorable FOH Volume Variance = Budgeted FOH > Applied FOH. Applied FOH is based on actual output. If actual output < planned, less FOH is applied (absorbed), leaving an unfavorable variance.

3-5.q: The Total Overhead Flexible Budget Variance Excludes which component?
3-5.e: Total OH Flexible Budget Variance = VOH Spending + VOH Efficiency + FOH Spending. It Excludes the FOH Production Volume Variance because volume variance is a planning issue (capacity), not an efficiency issue.

3-5.q: VOH Spending Variance = (AP − SP) × Aq. AP = $4.20/Dlh, SP = $4.00/Dlh, Aq = 3,000 Dlh. Variance =
3-5.e: VOH Spending Variance = ($4.20 − $4.00) × 3,000 = $0.20 × 3,000 = $600 Unfavorable (actual overhead rate per Dlh was higher than standard).

3-5.q: The FOH Production Volume Variance is associated with:
3-5.e: FOH Volume Variance reflects Capacity Planning and Utilization. It shows whether the company used its planned production capacity. It has No connection to actual spending efficiency — it's purely a volume/capacity issue.

3-5.q: Standard VOH rate = $3/Dlh. Standard Dlh = 2 hrs/unit. Actual production = 1,000 units. Actual Dlh = 2,100. VOH Efficiency Variance =
3-5.e: Sq Allowed = 2 × 1,000 = 2,000 Dlh. VOH Efficiency Variance = (2,100 − 2,000) × $3 = 100 × $3 = $300 Unfavorable.

3-5.q: VOH Spending Variance = Actual VOH − (Standard VOH Rate × Actual Hours). Favorable when:
o: More hours were used than standard
Actual VOH incurred per hour < Standard VOH rate — spent Less on variable overhead than expected for hours worked
3-5.e: VOH Spending Variance is favorable when Actual VOH < (Standard VOH Rate × Actual Hours). The company incurred Less variable overhead per hour than the standard — e.g., lower utility rates, efficient use of indirect materials.

3-5.q: VOH Efficiency Variance = (AH − Sh Allowed) × Standard VOH Rate. It is Favorable when:
o: Actual VOH rate < Standard VOH rate
Actual Hours < Standard Hours Allowed — fewer base units (e.g., Dlh) used than standard
3-5.e: Favorable VOH Efficiency Variance when Actual Hours < Standard Hours Allowed. The allocation base (e.g., DL hours) was used More Efficiently than standard — less variable overhead was applied because fewer hours were needed.

3-5.q: The FOH Spending Variance measures:
o: Whether Actual fixed overhead differed from Budgeted fixed overhead
3-5.e: FOH Spending Variance = Actual FOH − Budgeted FOH. Did we actually Spend more or less on fixed overhead than we planned? Examples: higher-than-budgeted rent increases, insurance premiums, or management salaries.

3-5.q: Applied Overhead = Pohr × Actual Activity. Pohr = $10/Dlh, Actual Dlh = 4,500. Applied OH =
3-5.e: Applied OH = $10 × 4,500 = $45,000. This is the overhead Charged To Wip using the predetermined rate times actual activity.

3-5.q: Total FOH Variance = FOH Spending Variance + FOH Production Volume Variance. Spending = $3,000 U, Volume = $5,000 U. Total FOH Variance =
3-5.e: Total FOH Variance = $3,000 U + $5,000 U = $8,000 Unfavorable. Both spending and volume were unfavorable — incurred more fixed OH than budgeted And under-absorbed due to low production.

3-5.q: When actual production Exceeds planned production, the FOH Production Volume Variance is:
o: Favorable — More fixed overhead is absorbed/applied than budgeted
Zero — volume does not affect fixed costs
3-5.e: When actual production > planned: Applied FOH > Budgeted FOH. More fixed overhead is absorbed → Favorable Volume Variance. Over-production improves fixed cost absorption — fixed costs are spread over More units than planned.

3-5.q: Under the Two-Variance method for overhead, the variances are:
3-5.e: Two-Variance method: (1) Budget Variance (Controllable) = both spending and efficiency combined, (2) Production Volume Variance = capacity utilization. The two-variance method is simpler but less informative than the four-variance method.

3-5.q: Over-applied overhead means Applied OH > Actual OH. This results in:
o: Under-absorbed overhead — debit balance in Moh
Over-absorbed overhead — credit balance in Moh — products were over-costed
3-5.e: Over-Applied (over-absorbed): Applied > Actual → Credit balance in Moh control account. Products were charged More overhead than was actually incurred — they are over-costed. At year-end, COGS must be Decreased (or variance is allocated).

3-5.q: The FOH Volume Variance is also called the:
o: Capacity Utilization variance — shows whether planned capacity was used
3-5.e: FOH Volume Variance = Capacity Utilization variance. It shows whether the company used the production capacity assumed in setting the Pohr. Under-utilization → unfavorable (fixed costs not fully absorbed). Over-utilization → favorable.

3-5.q: Standard VOH Rate = $4/Dlh. Actual Dlh = 3,000. Sh Allowed = 2,800. Actual VOH = $12,200. VOH Total Variance =
3-5.e: Applied VOH = Standard Rate × Standard Hours Allowed = $4 × 2,800 = $11,200. (Applied always uses Standard hours allowed, not actual hours.) Total VOH Variance = Actual VOH − Applied VOH = $12,200 − $11,200 = $1,000 Unfavorable. Unfavorable because actual cost ($12,200) exceeded what should have been spent for actual output ($11,200).

3-6.q: Sales variances are based on units:
3-6.e: Sales variances are based on units Sold — they appear on the sales variance report related to revenue and contribution margin performance, not the production variance report.

3-6.q: Actual selling price = $55. Budgeted selling price = $50. Actual units sold = 4,000. Selling Price Variance =
3-6.e: Selling Price Variance = ($55 − $50) × 4,000 = $5 × 4,000 = $20,000 Favorable (sold at higher price than budget — more revenue than expected).

3-6.q: The Sales Volume Variance measures:
o: Whether the selling price differed from budget
The impact of selling more or fewer units than budgeted
3-6.e: Sales Volume Variance = (Actual Units − Budgeted Units) × Budgeted price. It isolates the Volume Effect — how profit changed because units sold differed from the budget.

3-6.q: Flexible Budget Variance + Sales Volume Variance =
3-6.e: Flexible Budget Variance + Sales Volume Variance = Total Static Budget Variance. The static budget variance is the total difference between actual and budget, which can be decomposed into price/efficiency (flexible budget) and volume (sales volume) components.

3-6.q: Budgeted sales = 5,000 units at $40. Actual sales = 4,500 units at $42. Sales Volume Variance =
3-6.e: Sales Volume Variance = (4,500 − 5,000) × $40 = −500 × $40 = −$20,000 = $20,000 Unfavorable (sold 500 fewer units than budgeted).

3-6.q: For the Selling Price Variance, a positive result is:
3-6.e: For Sales, a positive variance is Favorable — actual price > budget = more revenue. This is the Opposite of cost variances where positive = unfavorable. Revenue-based variances: higher actual = better = favorable.

3-6.q: The Sales Mix Variance for multiple products measures:
o: Whether the proportion of products sold differed from the budgeted mix
3-6.e: Sales Mix Variance = (WASP_AM − WASP_SM) × Total Aq. It measures whether the Proportion (mix) of products sold was different from the standard mix. Favorable = selling more of higher-Cm products.

3-6.q: Sales Quantity Variance + Sales Mix Variance =
3-6.e: For multiple products, the Sales Volume Variance splits into: Sales Quantity Variance (total units change) + Sales Mix Variance (proportion change) = Total Sales Volume Variance.

3-6.q: A Favorable Sales Mix Variance in a multi-product company means:
o: More of the High-Cm products were sold relative to the mix
Less total units were sold than budget
3-6.e: Favorable Sales Mix Variance = WASP_AM > WASP_SM. The actual mix shifted toward Higher contribution margin products, generating more profit per unit sold than the standard mix would have.

3-6.q: Variable cost variance for sales: Actual variable cost < budgeted variable cost per unit results in:
3-6.e: For variable Costs in sales analysis: actual cost Less than budget = Favorable (same as cost variances generally). Lower actual variable costs mean higher actual contribution margin than planned.

3-6.q: Selling Price Variance = (Actual SP − Budgeted SP) × Actual Units Sold. AP = $45, Bp = $48, Actual units = 3,000. Variance =
3-6.e: Spv = ($45 − $48) × 3,000 = −$3 × 3,000 = −$9,000 = $9,000 Unfavorable (sold at lower price than budgeted).

3-6.q: Sales Volume Variance = (Actual Units − Budgeted Units) × Budgeted Cm per unit. Actual = 6,000, Budget = 5,000, Bcm = $15. Svv =
3-6.e: Svv = (6,000 − 5,000) × $15 = 1,000 × $15 = $15,000 Favorable (sold 1,000 more units than budgeted).

3-6.q: A company raises its price and sells Fewer units. This would produce:
o: Favorable selling price variance + Unfavorable sales volume variance
3-6.e: Higher price → Favorable selling price variance. Fewer units sold → Unfavorable sales volume variance. These two effects work in opposite directions — the net effect on revenue depends on price elasticity.

3-6.q: The Flexible Budget Variance for Revenues = Actual Revenue − Flexible Budget Revenue. This measures:
o: Price Effect only — what price was actually charged vs standard, at the same volume
3-6.e: For revenues, the Flexible Budget Variance captures the Selling Price Variance — the difference between actual and standard (budgeted) price, holding volume constant (both use actual volume). Volume effect is captured in the Sales Volume Variance.

3-6.q: A favorable Sales Volume Variance means the company:
o: Sold at a higher price than budgeted
Sold More units than budgeted — more volume
3-6.e: Favorable Svv = Actual units > Budgeted units. The company sold More than planned. At the budgeted contribution margin, this generates more profit. The variance uses Budgeted Cm (not actual) to isolate the pure Volume effect.

3-6.q: Which variance is used to evaluate a sales manager's Pricing decisions?
o: Selling Price Variance — measures actual price vs budget price
3-6.e: The Selling Price Variance directly measures whether the sales force charged higher or lower prices than the budget assumed. This reflects pricing strategy effectiveness — negotiation skill, discounting behavior, and market positioning.

3-6.q: Total Static Budget Revenue Variance = Flexible Budget Variance + Sales Volume Variance. Fbv = $8,000 F, Svv = $12,000 U. Total =
3-6.e: Total = $8,000 F + $12,000 U = −$8,000 + $12,000 = −$4,000 = $4,000 Unfavorable. The favorable price was not enough to offset the unfavorable volume shortfall.

3-6.q: The Sales Volume Variance uses Budgeted contribution margin (not actual) because:
o: It isolates the Pure Volume Effect — using budgeted Cm removes cost efficiency changes from the sales volume measure
3-6.e: Using Budgeted Cm in Svv isolates the Volume Effect only — how much additional profit came from selling more/fewer units at standard profitability. If actual Cm were used, cost changes would contaminate the measurement of volume performance.

3-6.q: A company budgeted to sell 10,000 units but sold 9,000. Budgeted Cm per unit = $25. The Sales Volume Variance impact on profit is:
3-6.e: Sales Volume Variance = (Actual Units − Budgeted Units) × Budgeted Cm = (9,000 − 10,000) × $25 = −1,000 × $25 = −$25,000 = $25,000 Unfavorable. Selling 1,000 fewer units than planned reduced contribution margin by $25,000.

3-6.q: If actual Cm per unit = $18 and budgeted Cm = $20, with actual units = 4,000, the Flexible Budget Variance for Cm =
3-6.e: Fbv for Cm = (Actual Cm per unit − Budget Cm per unit) × Actual units = ($18 − $20) × 4,000 = −$2 × 4,000 = −$8,000 = $8,000 Unfavorable. Either the selling price was lower or variable costs were higher than standard.

3-7.q: Market Size Variance measures the impact of:
o: The company gaining or losing market share
The total industry market being larger or smaller than expected
3-7.e: Market Size Variance = the effect of the Total Industry Market being different from expected. It is an External factor — the company cannot control overall market demand. Favorable = market grew more than expected.

3-7.q: Market Share Variance measures:
o: Whether the company captured more or less of the market than planned
3-7.e: Market Share Variance = the effect of the company's Market Share being different from expected. It is an Internal/Competitive factor — the company's ability to compete effectively for market customers.

3-7.q: Market Size Variance + Market Share Variance =
o: Sales Volume Variance (or Sales Quantity Variance)
3-7.e: Msv + Mshv = Sales Quantity Variance (multi-product) or Sales Volume Variance (single product). Together they explain Why volume differed from plan — was it the market or was it the company's competitive position?

3-7.q: A Favorable Market Share Variance means:
o: The company lost customers to competitors
The company captured a Larger share of the market than planned
3-7.e: Favorable Mshv = Actual Market Share > Expected Market Share. The company performed Better competitively — it captured a larger proportion of total market sales than planned.

3-7.q: A company exceeds its sales budget because the market grew, but its market share fell. This indicates:
o: Only good news — budget was exceeded
Favorable Msv but Unfavorable Mshv — external growth masked competitive weakness
3-7.e: Favorable Msv (market grew) + Unfavorable Mshv (lost share) = budget exceeded only due to market tailwinds. Despite missing share goals, the growing market lifted all sales. This is a Warning Sign about competitive position.

3-7.q: An Unfavorable Market Size Variance indicates:
o: The total industry market contracted below expectations
The company's selling prices were too low
3-7.e: Unfavorable Msv = Actual Market Size < Expected Market Size. The total Industry Demand was Lower than planned. This is an external factor — even with perfect competitive execution, fewer total industry sales exist.

3-7.q: Market variances are calculated using:
o: Weighted Average Standard Cm per unit for Standard Mix (WASCMP_SM)
3-7.e: Market variances use the Weighted Average Standard Contribution Margin per unit for the Standard Mix. Using Cm (not just price) ensures the variances reflect profitability impact, not just revenue.

3-7.q: Which is the Internal performance indicator between the two market variances?
3-7.e: Market Share Variance is Internal — it reflects how well the company competed for customers. The company can influence its market share through better products, pricing, service, and marketing. Market Size is external and largely uncontrollable.

3-7.q: Expected market share = 20%. Actual market share = 18%. This leads to:
3-7.e: Actual market share (18%) < Expected market share (20%) = Unfavorable Market Share Variance. The company captured a Smaller proportion of the market than planned — competitive underperformance.

3-7.q: Market variances are most useful for companies that:
o: Compete in measurable markets where industry data is available
3-7.e: Market variances require External Market Data (actual and expected industry size and market share). They are most useful for companies competing in markets where industry sales data is available and reliable, enabling meaningful external vs internal performance analysis.

3-7.q: Market Share Variance formula = (Actual Market Share % − Budgeted Market Share %) × Actual Market Size × Budgeted Cm per unit. A company's actual share is 12%, budgeted share is 10%. This produces a:
o: Favorable Market Share Variance — captured More market share than planned
3-7.e: Actual share (12%) > Budgeted share (10%) = Favorable Market Share Variance. The company performed Better Competitively than planned — won a larger proportion of total market sales.

3-7.q: Market Size Variance is Favorable when:
o: The total industry market was Larger than expected — external tailwind
3-7.e: Favorable Market Size Variance = actual total market size > expected market size. The industry grew More than forecasted. This is an External factor — beneficial for the company but not due to internal effort or competitive performance.

3-7.q: Why is it important to Separate market size and market share variances?
o: To distinguish Internal Competitive performance (share) from External market forces (size)
3-7.e: Separating them reveals whether sales volume changes were due to External forces (market grew or shrank) or Internal competitive performance (won or lost market share). A company can't control market size but Can influence its share — different implications for management action.

3-7.q: A company's actual sales volume = 12,000 units. Budgeted sales = 10,000. Actual market size = 120,000. Budgeted market share = 10%. Company's Actual market share =
3-7.e: Actual Market Share = Actual company sales / Actual total market = 12,000 / 120,000 = 10%. Even though the company sold More units, its Market Share stayed at 10% — the entire market grew proportionally.

3-7.q: An Unfavorable Market Share Variance indicates:
o: The company won More share than planned
The company won Less market share than planned — a competitive weakness
3-7.e: Unfavorable Market Share Variance = Actual Share < Budgeted Share. The company failed to capture its planned proportion of total market sales. This signals a Competitive Weakness — competitors may be gaining ground.

3-7.q: The Sales Quantity Variance (multi-product) is split into:
o: Market Size and Market Share variances
3-7.e: Sales Quantity Variance = Market Size Variance + Market Share Variance. The Sales Quantity Variance measures the volume effect (same as Svv for single product). Splitting further reveals whether the volume change was due to market forces or competitive performance.

3-7.q: A company beats its sales budget Only because the market grew, while its market share Fell. This is:
o: A Warning Sign — external growth Masked a competitive loss
3-7.e: This pattern = Favorable Market Size + Unfavorable Market Share. The company Lost competitive ground but the rising tide of market growth hid it. Management needs to address the declining share or it will hurt results when market growth slows.

3-7.q: Market variance analysis is Most useful for companies in:
o: Competitive Markets where understanding market share vs market growth is strategically important
3-7.e: Market variance analysis is most valuable in Competitive markets where the distinction between market-driven and company-driven performance matters. It helps separate external environmental effects from internal competitive execution — essential for strategic management.

3-7.q: Sales Quantity Variance differs from Sales Volume Variance in that Quantity Variance:
o: Uses actual mix rather than budgeted mix for multi-product companies
3-7.e: For Multi-Product companies: Sales Volume Variance = total volume effect using actual mix. Sales Quantity Variance = volume effect at Budgeted Mix — isolating the quantity effect from any mix shift. The difference between them = Sales Mix Variance.

3-7.q: An Unfavorable Market Size Variance and Favorable Market Share Variance means:
o: The market Contracted but the company Outperformed competitors — gained share despite industry headwinds
The market grew but the company lost share
Both external and competitive performance were poor
3-7.e: Unfavorable Msv (market shrunk) + Favorable Mshv (gained share) = company is performing Well Competitively even as the overall market contracts. This is actually encouraging — winning in a shrinking market demonstrates competitive strength.

3-8.q: An Investment Center manager is Primarily evaluated using:
3-8.e: Investment Center: managers control revenues, costs, And the investment base. Evaluated using ROI (return on investment) and/or Residual Income (Ri) — measures that capture both profit And the assets deployed.

3-8.q: A Cost Center manager is responsible for:
3-8.e: Cost Center: manager controls Costs Only. Performance measured by cost efficiency (actual vs budget). Examples: production department, IT department. Not responsible for revenues or investment decisions.

3-8.q: The key principle in manager evaluation is:
o: Hold managers accountable for All company costs
Evaluate managers Only on items they Can Control
Use ROI for all manager types
3-8.e: Key Principle: Evaluate managers Only on Controllable items — costs and revenues they can directly influence. Including non-controllable items (e.g., allocated corporate overhead) creates unfair and demotivating evaluations.

3-8.q: A common cost is Best defined as:
o: A cost shared by multiple departments based on usage
A cost that cannot be traced to a specific segment on a cause-effect basis and remains if the segment is eliminated
3-8.e: Common cost = cannot be traced to a segment based on cause-and-effect And remains even if the segment is eliminated. Example: CEO salary exists regardless of which division is dropped. Different from shared services which Can be allocated by usage.

3-8.q: The Stand-Alone method of allocating common costs:
o: Ranks units by size and charges the largest first
Allocates based on each unit's proportional usage or revenues
3-8.e: Stand-alone method: allocate based on each unit's Proportional usage, revenues, or capacity — treating each unit as if it were independent. Considered Fair because each unit pays a proportional share.

3-8.q: Shared Services costs (IT, payroll, maintenance) differ from Common Costs because:
o: They Can be allocated based on measurable usage/cost drivers
They are excluded from all reports
3-8.e: Shared Services have measurable Cost Drivers or usage — IT hours used, payroll transactions, maintenance hours. These can be allocated to user departments based on actual usage. Common costs lack this cause-effect relationship.

3-8.q: A Profit Center manager is responsible for:
3-8.e: Profit Center: manager controls Both revenues and costs, making them responsible for Profitability. Evaluated on profit margin or contribution margin. Examples: store division, product line.

3-8.q: The incremental method of common cost allocation:
o: Gives all costs to the smallest unit
Ranks units — primary unit bears costs as if sole user; incremental units get remainder
3-8.e: Incremental method: Rank units (largest usually = primary). Primary unit is allocated costs as if it were the Only user. Remaining costs are allocated to the next (incremental) unit, and so on. Advantage: protects smaller/newer units.

3-8.q: A department is eliminated. Which cost continues?
o: Direct costs traceable to the department
3-8.e: Common Costs continue to exist even after a segment is eliminated — they are Not caused by the segment and cannot be avoided by eliminating it. This is a key consideration in segment elimination decisions.

3-8.q: Unit performance and manager performance are:
o: Related but Separate — unit performance includes non-controllable factors
The same if ROI is used
3-8.e: Unit performance = All traceable results (including non-controllable factors). Manager performance = only Controllable items. Example: a turnaround manager of a struggling division should not be judged solely on short-term unit results.

3-8.q: A Revenue Center manager has authority over:
o: Revenues and associated selling costs only
3-8.e: Revenue Center: manager has authority over Revenues (and selling costs). No responsibility for manufacturing costs or capital investment. Evaluated on whether revenues and selling expenses meet budget.

3-8.q: A Profit Center manager has authority over:
o: Both revenues And costs but Not capital investment
Capital investment but not operating costs
3-8.e: Profit Center: manager controls Both revenues and costs — responsible for the resulting profit margin. Does Not have authority over capital investment decisions. Evaluated on profit vs budget. Example: regional division with revenue and cost control but no capital authority.

3-8.q: The Incremental method of allocating common costs:
o: Ranks users — the Primary user bears the cost first, others get remaining costs
Allocates based on budgeted usage only
3-8.e: Incremental Method: ranks users — the Primary (first) user is charged the full stand-alone cost of that user level; Incremental users are charged only the additional cost their participation adds. Can be seen as less fair than stand-alone.

3-8.q: Which cost is traceable to a specific segment And would disappear if that segment were eliminated?
o: Direct (traceable) Fixed Cost of that segment — e.g., segment manager salary
3-8.e: Direct (traceable) fixed costs of a segment are caused by the segment's existence — they Disappear if the segment closes. Examples: segment-specific advertising, segment manager salary. These are included in Segment Margin but excluded from Controllable Margin.

3-8.q: A Transfer Price is set between:
o: Two Divisions Within The Same Company for goods/services one provides to the other
3-8.e: Transfer Price = the price at which one Division of a company sells to Another Division. It affects each division's reported profit but has no effect on consolidated company profit (internal transfers are eliminated in consolidation).

3-8.q: Controllable Margin = Contribution Margin − Controllable Fixed Costs. Cm = $200,000, Controllable Fixed Costs = $80,000. Controllable Margin =
3-8.e: Controllable Margin = $200,000 − $80,000 = $120,000. This measures Short-Term Manager Performance — only what the manager can control. Non-controllable traceable fixed costs and common costs are Not deducted here.

3-8.q: Segment Margin = Controllable Margin − Non-controllable Traceable Fixed Costs. Controllable Margin = $120,000, Non-ctrl Fixed = $40,000. Segment Margin =
3-8.e: Segment Margin = $120,000 − $40,000 = $80,000. This measures Long-Term Segment Profitability — whether the segment generates enough value to cover all costs specific to its existence. Used for keep/close/expand decisions.

3-8.q: Common fixed costs should be Excluded from individual segment evaluations because:
o: They are too small to matter
They Exist Regardless of the segment — allocated amounts are arbitrary and create misleading segment profitability
3-8.e: Common fixed costs continue even if a segment is closed (e.g., CEO salary allocated to all divisions). Including them in segment evaluation Distorts performance — the segment cannot control or eliminate these costs. They appear only at the corporate total level.

3-8.q: An Investment Center manager who is evaluated only on ROI might Reject a profitable project if:
o: The project has a positive NPV
The project's ROI is Lower than the manager's current division ROI — even if above hurdle rate
The project has a long payback period
3-8.e: ROI Dysfunction: if a manager's current ROI is 25% and a new project ROI is 18% (but above the 12% company hurdle rate), the project Lowers the manager's average ROI → manager rejects it. This is bad for the company but rational for the manager under ROI evaluation.

3-8.q: The Stand-Alone cost allocation method is considered Fair because:
o: It charges the largest user the most
Each entity pays its proportional share as if it were the only user
3-8.e: Stand-alone method: each user is allocated costs based on their Proportional share of usage, revenues, or capacity — as if each were operating independently. Considered Fair because each user pays in proportion to their benefit received.

3-9.q: The Contribution Income Statement classifies costs by:
3-9.e: Cis classifies costs by Behavior — variable vs fixed. This is different from the traditional income statement which classifies by function (manufacturing costs in COGS; S&A as period costs). Cis separates fixed costs below the contribution margin line.

3-9.q: Manufacturing Contribution Margin (Mcm) =
3-9.e: Mcm = Net Revenues − Variable Manufacturing Costs (DM + DL + Variable Moh). It shows how much revenue remains after covering Variable manufacturing costs — before non-manufacturing variable costs or any fixed costs.

3-9.q: The Controllable Margin is used to evaluate:
o: Short-term Manager performance on controllable items
3-9.e: Controllable Margin = Cm − Controllable Fixed Costs. Measures Short-Term Manager Performance — only costs and revenues the manager can Control within the evaluation period. Excludes non-controllable fixed costs.

3-9.q: Non-controllable traceable fixed costs are Included in Segment Margin because:
o: Managers control them in the long run
They are eliminated if the segment is closed — relevant to segment decisions
3-9.e: Non-controllable traceable fixed costs Are Eliminated if the segment closes — they are traceable to (caused by) the segment. Including them in Segment Margin provides a True picture of segment profitability for keep/close decisions.

3-9.q: Segment Margin is used for:
o: Long-term decisions about keeping, expanding, or eliminating a segment
3-9.e: Segment Margin = profitability after All traceable fixed costs. Used for Long-Term strategic decisions — whether to keep, expand, or Close the segment. A negative segment margin suggests the segment destroys value.

3-9.q: Contribution Margin (Level 2) =
3-9.e: Cm = Mcm − Variable Non-Manufacturing Costs (variable selling, marketing, admin). It represents funds available to cover All Fixed Costs after all variable costs (manufacturing and non-manufacturing) have been deducted.

3-9.q: A segment should Generally be retained if its:
o: Segment Margin is positive (covers all traceable fixed costs)
Only if ROI exceeds the hurdle rate
3-9.e: A segment should be retained if its Segment Margin is positive — meaning it covers all its traceable fixed costs and contributes to covering common (corporate) costs. A negative segment margin suggests dropping it may improve overall profitability.

3-9.q: Corporate allocated overhead is:
o: Excluded from both segment and manager evaluation
3-9.e: Corporate/common overhead Cannot be traced to any specific segment — it exists regardless of which segments operate. It is Excluded from both segment and manager evaluation. However, company must ensure All operations together cover these costs.

3-9.q: Non-manufacturing variable costs are deducted at which level?
3-9.e: Non-manufacturing variable costs (variable selling, marketing, admin) are deducted at Level 2 to arrive at Contribution Margin (Cm). Level 1 (Mcm) only deducts variable manufacturing costs.

3-9.q: Controllable fixed costs in the Cis are those the manager can influence:
3-9.e: Controllable fixed costs = fixed costs the manager can influence within Less Than One Year (short-term). Examples: hiring/firing supervisors, discretionary advertising, segment-specific promotions. Long-term fixed costs (leases, depreciation) are typically non-controllable.

3-9.q: The Main difference between the Contribution Income Statement and the Traditional Income Statement is:
o: Cis separates costs by Behavior (variable vs fixed); Traditional separates by Function (product vs period)
3-9.e: Cis = costs separated by Behavior (variable vs fixed). Traditional = costs separated by Function (manufacturing costs in COGS; selling & admin as period costs). Both arrive at the same net income but organize costs differently — Cis is more useful for internal decisions.

3-9.q: Net Revenue = $500,000. Variable Manufacturing Costs = $200,000. Manufacturing Contribution Margin (Mcm) =
3-9.e: Mcm = Net Revenue − Variable Manufacturing Costs = $500,000 − $200,000 = $300,000. Mcm shows profitability after covering Variable Manufacturing Costs only — before non-manufacturing variable costs or any fixed costs.

3-9.q: Mcm − Variable Non-Manufacturing Costs = Cm. Mcm = $300,000, Variable S&A = $60,000. Contribution Margin =
3-9.e: Cm = Mcm − Variable Non-Manufacturing Costs = $300,000 − $60,000 = $240,000. Contribution Margin covers All variable costs (manufacturing + non-manufacturing) and shows what remains to cover All fixed costs and generate profit.

3-9.q: Which income measure is Most useful for the Keep Or Close segment decision?
o: Segment Margin — after all traceable fixed costs
3-9.e: Segment Margin (after all traceable fixed costs) is used for Keep/Close decisions. If Segment Margin < 0, closing eliminates the contribution And the traceable fixed costs — net effect is positive. Common costs are excluded as they remain regardless.

3-9.q: Which income measure is Most useful for Short-Term Manager performance evaluation?
o: Controllable Margin — only includes items the manager controls
3-9.e: Controllable Margin is used for Short-Term manager evaluation — it includes only costs and revenues the manager can Directly Control. Non-controllable traceable fixed costs and common costs are excluded — fair evaluation requires controllability.

3-9.q: Common fixed costs appear on the Contribution Income Statement:
o: Only in the variable cost section
Below all segment margins — as a deduction from total segment margins to get company operating income
3-9.e: Common fixed costs cannot be traced to individual segments — they appear Below all segment margins as a company-wide deduction. Format: Sum of Segment Margins − Common Fixed Costs = Company Operating Income.

3-9.q: A segment's Controllable Margin = $50,000. Non-controllable Traceable Fixed Costs = $30,000. Segment Margin =
3-9.e: Segment Margin = Controllable Margin − Non-controllable Traceable Fixed Costs = $50,000 − $30,000 = $20,000.

3-9.q: Variable non-manufacturing costs include which of the following?
o: Variable Selling Commissions based on units sold
3-9.e: Variable non-manufacturing costs = costs that vary with sales/production but are Not manufacturing costs. Examples: Variable Sales Commissions (per unit sold), variable delivery costs, variable advertising tied to sales. These are deducted from Mcm to get Contribution Margin.

3-9.q: If a segment's Segment Margin is Negative, management should consider:
o: Investigating whether closing the segment would Eliminate the traceable fixed costs and improve total company profit
3-9.e: Negative Segment Margin is a Signal to investigate — it means the segment doesn't cover its own traceable costs. If closing eliminates those traceable fixed costs (and common costs remain), closing Improves company profit. But other factors (customer relationships, strategic value) must also be considered.

3-9.q: The Cis format starting from the top is:
o: Sales → COGS → Gross Profit → S&A → Operating Income
Sales → Variable Mfg → Mcm → Variable Non-Mfg → Cm → Controllable Fixed → Controllable Margin → Non-ctrl Fixed → Segment Margin
Sales → All Fixed Costs → Operating Income
3-9.e: Full Cis hierarchy: Sales − Variable Manufacturing Costs = Mcm − Variable Non-Manufacturing Costs = Cm − Controllable Fixed Costs = Controllable Margin − Non-controllable Traceable Fixed = Segment Margin. Common fixed costs deducted last.

3-10.q: A transfer price affects which of the following?
o: Divisional reported profits and manager performance evaluation
3-10.e: At the Consolidated level, internal transfers are eliminated — no effect on total company net income. However, transfer prices directly affect each Division's reported revenues and costs, which impacts manager performance evaluation.

3-10.q: The Market Price transfer method is preferred when:
o: An external market exists for the intermediate product
The selling division has excess capacity
Divisions are in different countries only
3-10.e: Market price is preferred when an External Market Exists for the intermediate product. It is the most Objective and Fair method — approximates arm's-length pricing and is defensible to tax authorities.

3-10.q: The general guideline for minimum transfer price when the selling division is at Full Capacity is:
o: Variable cost + Cm lost from forgone external sales
3-10.e: At Full Capacity: min transfer price = Outlay Cost (variable cost) + Opportunity Cost (Cm from external sales foregone). The selling division must be compensated for the profit it gives up by Not selling externally.

3-10.q: Transferring at Variable Cost is most appropriate when:
o: The seller is at full capacity
3-10.e: Variable cost transfer price works well when the selling division has Excess Capacity — no external sales are being foregone, so opportunity cost = 0. The buying division gets the internal benefit of the idle capacity.

3-10.q: Dual-Rate transfer pricing gives the selling division market price and buying division variable cost. Its main Disadvantage is:
o: Divisional profits no longer add up to corporate total
It is too complex to set up
3-10.e: Dual-rate: selling division records market price revenue; buying division records variable cost. Both divisions look profitable. But the divisional profits are Inflated and No Longer Add To the corporate total — creating reconciliation issues.

3-10.q: Under US law (Irc §482), multinational transfer prices must be set at:
o: Arm's-length prices (as if between unrelated parties)
3-10.e: Irc §482 requires Arm'S-Length pricing — prices must be set as if the divisions were Independent, Unrelated parties. This prevents artificial income shifting to low-tax jurisdictions. Most countries have similar rules.

3-10.q: Goal congruence in transfer pricing means:
o: Both divisions agree on the price
Managers make decisions that benefit Both their division And the overall company
Prices are set by a formula
All divisions use the same method
3-10.e: Goal Congruence = manager decisions benefit Both their own division And The Overall Company. A good transfer pricing system aligns divisional incentives with company-wide objectives — preventing suboptimal internal decisions.

3-10.q: Full-cost transfer pricing Disadvantage is that:
o: It is too complex to calculate
It is not accepted for tax
Including fixed costs may Mislead make-vs-buy decisions
It benefits only the buying division
3-10.e: Full-cost includes Allocated Fixed Overhead. The buying division sees a higher cost, which may lead it to prefer external purchasing even when internal transfer is optimal for the company as a whole — misleading decisions.

3-10.q: If the selling division has excess capacity, the Minimum transfer price should be:
3-10.e: Excess capacity = no external sales foregone = opportunity cost = 0. Minimum transfer price = Outlay (Variable) Cost only. Any price above variable cost contributes to covering the seller's fixed costs — beneficial to the company.

3-10.q: Negotiated transfer pricing's main Disadvantage is:
o: It is always unfavorable to the buyer
Results may reflect negotiating skills rather than economic efficiency
3-10.e: Negotiated prices may reflect which division Negotiated Better rather than what is economically efficient. A stronger negotiator may extract a favorable price that is not in the company's best interest overall.

3-10.q: The Minimum transfer price formula for any situation: Min Tp =
o: Outlay Cost + Opportunity Cost of the selling division
3-10.e: Minimum Tp = Outlay Cost (variable cost of producing the unit) + Opportunity Cost (profit from best alternative use of capacity — e.g., Cm from external sales foregone). This ensures the selling division is at least as well off with the internal transfer.

3-10.q: When the selling division has Excess Capacity and No external market, the minimum transfer price approaches:
o: Variable Cost Only — opportunity cost is zero since no external sales are foregone
3-10.e: With Excess Capacity and no external sales being displaced: Opportunity Cost = 0. Min Tp = Variable Cost + $0 = Variable Cost. The selling division has idle capacity — any contribution above variable cost improves its position.

3-10.q: A cost-plus transfer price is set as:
o: Standard variable cost + a markup percentage for profit
Total Cost (variable + fixed) + a profit margin
3-10.e: Cost-Plus transfer pricing = full cost (variable + fixed) + a profit markup. Commonly used when no external market price exists. Limitation: it may transfer a selling division's inefficiencies to the buying division and can lead to suboptimal decisions.

3-10.q: A Negotiated transfer price is preferred because:
o: It always equals the market price
It Balances Division Autonomy with corporate goal congruence — managers negotiate a mutually acceptable price
It is the simplest to compute
3-10.e: Negotiated Tp allows divisions to negotiate based on their own cost structures and market alternatives. Advantages: respects Division Autonomy and can lead to Goal Congruent decisions. Disadvantages: time-consuming; may lead to disputes; management may need to arbitrate.

3-10.q: International transfer pricing is influenced by taxation because:
o: Companies can shift profits to Low-Tax jurisdictions by adjusting transfer prices between subsidiaries
Transfer prices always must equal variable cost
3-10.e: Multinational companies can use transfer prices to Shift Profits between countries — pricing transfers high to subsidiaries in high-tax countries (reducing their profit) and low to subsidiaries in low-tax countries (increasing their profit). Tax authorities regulate this via arm's-length pricing requirements.

3-10.q: Arm's-length pricing (required for international tax) means:
o: Setting transfer prices as if the two divisions were Independent parties dealing at market rates
3-10.e: Arm'S-Length = prices set as if the two divisions were Unrelated, Independent companies negotiating in the open market. This is the international tax standard (Irc §482 in the US, Oecd guidelines globally) designed to prevent profit-shifting for tax avoidance.

3-10.q: Goal congruence in transfer pricing means the transfer price should:
o: Maximize the selling division's profit always
Minimize the buying division's costs always
Motivate Both divisions to make decisions that Maximize Total Company Profit
3-10.e: Goal Congruence: the transfer price should encourage both divisions to make decisions that Benefit The Entire Company — not just their own division. If a transfer price leads a division to reject a profitable company-wide opportunity, goal congruence is violated.

3-10.q: Two-part transfer pricing charges the buying division:
o: A flat fee regardless of usage
A Lump Sum (for fixed cost recovery) + Variable Cost per unit transferred
Full cost with a fixed markup
3-10.e: Two-Part Tp: buying division pays a Lump Sum fee (covers the selling division's fixed costs) + Variable Cost per unit transferred. This allows the selling division to recover fixed costs while charging the buying division at marginal cost — promoting efficient usage decisions.

3-10.q: Under a market-price transfer, the selling division earns:
o: The Same profit it would earn from an external sale — no advantage or disadvantage to transferring internally
3-10.e: Market Price transfer: selling division receives the external market price — same as an external sale. It is indifferent between internal and external customers. This is the most Objective method and maintains divisional autonomy.

3-10.q: Dual-rate transfer pricing means:
o: Two divisions use different GAAP standards
The Selling Division records the Market Price while the Buying Division records Variable Cost for the same transfer
3-10.e: Dual-Rate: Selling division records Market Price (credit revenue), Buying division records Variable Cost (debit expense). Both benefit — selling looks profitable, buying avoids full price. Disadvantage: divisional profits Inflate and don't add to corporate total.

3-11.q: ROI = Operating Income / Assets Employed. Division A: Income = $100,000, Assets = $500,000. ROI =
A division's current ROI = 25%. A new project has ROI = 18% but exceeds the company's 12% hurdle rate. A manager using ROI metric will likely:
o: Accept the project — it's above hurdle rate
Reject the project — it would Lower the division's overall ROI
Accept it only if Ri is positive
3-11.e: ROI Dysfunction: a manager with 25% current ROI will Reject an 18% project because it lowers the division average — even though 18% > 12% hurdle rate and benefits the company. This is the key limitation of ROI as a sole metric.

3-11.q: Residual Income (Ri) = Operating Income − (Assets × Rrr). Income = $80,000, Assets = $400,000, Rrr = 15%. Ri =
3-11.e: Ri = $80,000 − ($400,000 × 15%) = $80,000 − $60,000 = $20,000. Positive Ri means the division earns more than the required return on its assets.

3-11.q: The Key Advantage of Residual Income over ROI is that Ri:
o: Encourages managers to accept all investments that exceed the Rrr (no ROI dysfunctionality)
Is not affected by accounting policies
3-11.e: Ri encourages accepting All projects where project return > Rrr (Ri > 0). It eliminates the ROI problem where a manager rejects a good project because it would lower their current high ROI percentage.

3-11.q: The Main Limitation of Residual Income is:
o: Larger divisions naturally have higher Ri — not comparable across divisions of different sizes
It ignores the cost of capital
3-11.e: Ri is Not Scale-Neutral — a larger division has a higher asset base and generates more absolute profit. Its Ri will naturally be larger than a smaller division's, making direct comparison across differently-sized divisions misleading.

3-11.q: The Required Rate of Return (Rrr) is most commonly based on:
3-11.e: Rrr = typically based on WACC — the weighted average cost of the company's debt and equity financing. It represents the minimum return needed to satisfy all capital providers. Adjusted upward for higher-risk investments.

3-11.q: Which is an advantage of ROI as a performance measure?
o: Encourages acceptance of all beneficial projects
Allows comparison across divisions and against hurdle rates
3-11.e: ROI's main advantage: Comparability — it is a Rate (%), so it can be compared across divisions of different sizes and against the Rrr/hurdle rate. A 20% ROI can be compared to a 15% hurdle rate regardless of division size.

3-11.q: Different depreciation methods affect ROI and Ri because:
o: They change asset book value and period expense — affecting both income and assets employed
3-11.e: Depreciation affects: (1) Net income (expense in the period) and (2) Net book value of assets (accumulated depreciation reduces assets). Both income And the denominator (assets) change, making ROI and Ri sensitive to depreciation method choice.

3-11.q: Multinational performance evaluation is complicated by:
o: Currency exchange rates, inflation differences, and different regulatory environments
3-11.e: Multinational evaluation challenges: Currency exchange rates affect reported profits, Inflation rates vary by country, different Tariffs and Regulations affect costs. Solution: adjust metrics for local conditions to enable fair comparison.

3-11.q: A division with ROI = 30% receives a proposed project with expected ROI = 22%. The company's hurdle rate = 15%. For the company, this project is:
o: Good — 22% > 15% hurdle rate; increases company value
3-11.e: For the Company: 22% > 15% hurdle rate → project creates value. For the Division Manager using ROI: 22% < 30% current ROI → manager is incentivized to Reject. This illustrates the ROI dysfunctionality. Ri would correctly incentivize acceptance.

3-11.q: ROI can be decomposed using DuPont analysis into:
o: Profit Margin × Asset Turnover (ROI = Net Income/Sales × Sales/Assets)
3-11.e: DuPont ROI = Profit Margin (Income/Sales) × Asset Turnover (Sales/Assets). This reveals two ways to Improve ROI: (1) increase profit per dollar of sales (margin), or (2) generate more sales from the same assets (turnover). Different strategies imply different improvement levers.

3-11.q: A division can improve its ROI by:
o: Increasing assets while holding profit constant
Increasing Net Income (higher margin) Or Decreasing Assets Employed (better asset turnover) — or both
3-11.e: ROI = Income / Assets. Improve ROI by: (1) Increasing Income (reduce costs, increase prices, increase volume) while holding assets steady, Or (2) Reducing Assets Employed (dispose of idle assets, reduce Wc needs) while holding income steady.

3-11.q: Net book value (depreciated cost) is Most commonly used as the asset base for ROI because:
o: It is required by GAAP for external reporting
It is consistent with values on the Balance Sheet — easiest to obtain from financial records
3-11.e: Net Book Value = original cost minus accumulated depreciation. Most commonly used because it matches the Balance Sheet — readily available from accounting records. Limitation: as assets depreciate, ROI Automatically Improves even with unchanged performance (lower denominator).

3-11.q: Economic Value Added (EVA) = Nopat − (WACC × Invested Capital). EVA is Positive when:
o: Nopat exceeds the cost of capital charge — the company creates value above investor requirements
3-11.e: EVA > 0 means the company generates returns Above its cost of capital — it is Creating Shareholder Value. EVA is conceptually similar to Ri but uses Nopat (after-tax operating profit) and invested capital at market values, making it more theoretically rigorous.

3-11.q: Residual Income = Operating Income − (Assets × Required Rate of Return). Division: Income = $50,000, Assets = $250,000, Rrr = 15%. Ri =
3-11.e: Ri = $50,000 − ($250,000 × 15%) = $50,000 − $37,500 = $12,500. Positive Ri means the division earns More than investors require — creating value.

3-11.q: Using Gross book value (original cost, no depreciation) as the ROI asset base:
o: Automatically improves ROI as assets age
Avoids the distortion of automatic ROI improvement as assets depreciate — more stable over asset lifetime
Is always lower than net book value
3-11.e: Gross book value stays Constant regardless of depreciation age. This avoids the automatic ROI improvement that occurs under net book value as assets age (lower denominator → higher ROI with no change in performance). More stable comparison over time.

3-11.q: Asset Turnover = Sales / Total Assets. Sales = $800,000, Total Assets = $500,000. Asset Turnover =
3-11.e: Asset Turnover = $800,000 / $500,000 = 1.6 times. The company generates $1.60 of sales for every $1 of assets employed. Higher turnover = more efficient use of assets.

3-11.q: A manager using Ri as a performance metric would Accept a project if:
o: Project generates positive Ri (return > Rrr) — it adds to divisional Ri
3-11.e: Ri metric: accept any project with Positive Ri (project return > Rrr). Unlike ROI which creates a dysfunctional threshold, Ri Encourages accepting all positive-Ri projects regardless of the division's current Ri level. This promotes Goal Congruence.

3-11.q: The Main limitation of ROI that Ri overcomes is:
o: ROI managers may Reject profitable projects that would lower their ROI percentage even if the project exceeds the company's hurdle rate — Ri eliminates this bias
3-11.e: ROI's key flaw: managers with high current ROI reject Good projects (above hurdle rate) because they'd dilute the ROI average. Ri overcomes this — Any project with Ri > 0 is accepted because it adds to the absolute Ri figure regardless of current level.

3-11.q: Profit Margin (for DuPont ROI) = Net Income / Sales. Net Income = $60,000, Sales = $400,000. Profit Margin =
3-11.e: Profit Margin = $60,000 / $400,000 = 0.15 = 15%. This shows that for every $1 of sales, the company earns $0.15 of profit after all costs.

3-12.q: The Balanced Scorecard was developed by:
